/lessons/basics:
The Greek alphabet consists of 24 distinct graphemes. It is the foundation of Western writing. Mastery of these
        symbols is the first step to literacy.
: Proper nouns and sentence starters are capitalized.

/lessons/double-letters:
In Modern Greek, certain combinations of two letters are used to
            represent a single sound. These are called

or
            "double letters". They are essential for correct pronunciation and spelling.

These combinations change their sound based on the letter that
            follows them.

                    consonants (β, γ, δ, ζ, λ, μ, ν, ρ).

                    (θ, κ, ξ, π, σ, τ, φ, χ, ψ).
/lessons/read:
Reading Greek is a process of assembling phonetic blocks
            (syllables). Unlike English, Greek is highly phonetic—once you know
            the sound of a letter or a "double letter", it almost always sounds
            the same.
Follow these examples to see how sounds combine to form words.
Treat every word as a series of distinct sounds. Notice how the

dictates where the voice rises. Without the accent mark, we wouldn't
                know which syllable to emphasize!
/lessons/vowels-consonants:
Understanding the classification of Greek letters into vowels and
            consonants is the final step before mastering the stress system.

Greek has seven vowels, although some represent the same sound due
            to historical phonetic evolution.
all make the exact same "ee" sound. This is called

Consonants are divided into categories based on how they are
            produced (voiced vs. voiceless), but for now, remember their forms:
Β, Γ, Δ, Ζ, Θ, Κ, Λ, Μ, Ν, Ξ, Π, Ρ, Σ, Τ, Φ, Χ, Ψ
Vowels are the carriers of the

. In Lesson 5,
            you will learn that the accent mark can only be placed above one of
            these seven vowels.
/lessons/accents: In Modern Greek, the accent mark (

) is a crucial orthographic feature that indicates which syllable
            of a word is stressed. Unlike English, where stress patterns can be
            ambiguous, Greek uses a visible mark to ensure correct
            pronunciation.

Only the last three syllables of a word
                can carry an accent.

Words with only one syllable generally
                do not take an accent (with a few grammatical exceptions).

The placement of the accent can change the entire meaning of a word.
            For example,
/lessons/punctuation:
Greek punctuation is very similar to English, but there are two
            critical differences that often confuse beginners: the
Functions exactly as in English. Ends a sentence.
Functions exactly as in English. Separates clauses.

The "Upper Point" functions like a colon or a semicolon in
                    English. It is a dot placed at the top of the line.

When typing in Greek, the semicolon key

/lessons/nouns:
Nouns in Modern Greek (Τα Ουσιαστικά) are complex entities governed by gender, case, and number. Gender is a
        fundamental property.

/lessons/nouns.style: display: grid; gridtemplatecolumns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(200px, 1fr)); gap: 2rem; margin: 2rem 0

/lessons/nouns: Never learn a noun in isolation. Always learn it alongside its definite article (ο, η, το).
/lessons/genders: Every Greek noun belongs to one of three grammatical categories: Masculine, Feminine, or Neuter.

/lessons/genders: While most -ος nouns are masculine, some are feminine (η οδός). The article is your only fail-safe guide.
/lessons/articles: In Greek, almost every noun is preceded by an

. The article tells us the gender, number, and case of the noun.
            There are two types:

This is used when referring to a specific item. In English, we only
            have "the", but in Greek, it changes based on gender.

In the plural, Masculine and Feminine share
                the same article (

This is used for non-specific items. There is no plural form for the
            indefinite article in Greek (similar to English—we don't say "a
            cars").
/lessons/cases: The role of a noun in a sentence is determined by its
. Modern Greek uses four primary cases.
/lessons/declension:
In English, words like "friend" usually stay the same whether they
            are the subject or the object of a sentence. In Greek, nouns are

. This means they change their ending based on their grammatical
            role. This process is called

Think of Greek as a "flexing" language. Just as you flex a
                muscle, words "flex" their endings to signal their function. If
                a word is the "doer" (subject), it has one ending; if it's the
                "receiver" (object), it has another.

In most Greek sentences, the following elements must agree and
            decline together:

Most nouns follow predictable patterns (Regular Nouns). However,
                some nouns are "indeclinable" (don't change) or follow their own
                unique rules. We will focus on the regular patterns first.

/lessons/articles-declension:
Just like nouns, the Greek definite article ("the") must be declined
            to match the case of the noun it describes. This is one of the most
            important patterns to memorize, as it often provides the clearest
            clue to a noun's case.

/lessons/pronouns:
Pronouns are words used in place of nouns to avoid repetition and
            make speech more fluid. Modern Greek has several categories of
            pronouns, most of which decline based on gender, number, and
            case—just like the nouns they replace.
In Modern Greek, subject pronouns (I, you, we) are often

because the verb ending already tells us who is performing the action.
                We only use them for emphasis or clarity.
/lessons/personal-pronouns:
The personal pronouns in Greek change based on the person (1st, 2nd,
            3rd) and their function in the sentence (Subject vs. Object).
These are used to represent the person performing the action.
Greek has two ways to say things like "me" or "him".

(clitics) are used
            in normal speech and usually attached to the verb.
/lessons/possessive-pronouns:
In Greek, possession is usually expressed using a "weak" form of the
            personal pronoun that follows the noun. To express "own" or for
            stronger emphasis, we use the word

These short words always follow the noun and never change their form
            based on the noun's case—they only match the person and number of
            the owner.

acts like an adjective and
            is used to say "mine" or "my own". It must match the gender of the object
            being possessed.

                before it. Think of it like saying "the mine own". Example:
/lessons/demonstrative-pronouns:
Demonstrative pronouns are used to point to specific objects or
            people, indicating their relative distance from the speaker.
Used for things near the speaker.
Used for things distant from the speaker.
Used to indicate quality or type.
He is not such a man.
Used to indicate quantity or degree.
When a demonstrative pronoun is used with a noun, the noun
/lessons/relative-pronouns:
Relative pronouns are used to connect clauses or refer back to a
            noun previously mentioned. The most common one is the indeclinable

The universal relative pronoun. It never changes its
                        form.
Used to refer to an unspecified thing or sentence.
/lessons/interrogative-pronouns:
Interrogative pronouns are used to ask questions. In Greek, almost
            all interrogative pronouns carry an accent mark to distinguish them
            from their non-interrogative counterparts.

Used to ask about identity. It declines like an
                        adjective.

The universal "what". It is indeclinable (stays the
                        same).
In Modern Greek, one of the few words that

keep their accent even when they are single-syllable are interrogative
                words (πώς, πού, τι can be an exception but usually keeps the context).
                The accent tells the reader "this is a question".
/lessons/indefinite-pronouns:
Indefinite pronouns refer to people or things in a non-specific way.
            Greek has a rich set of these pronouns, some of which are declinable
            and others which are stable (indeclinable).
Refering to an unknown specific person.
/lessons/reflexive-pronouns:
Reflexive pronouns are used when the subject and the object of a
            verb are the same person. In Greek, we use the phrase

In the plural, the word "self" (εαυτός) can either stay singular or
            become plural (εαυτοί).
Reflexive pronouns are usually used as objects. Example:
/lessons/definite-pronouns:
Definite pronouns in Greek are used to express identity ("the same")
            or exclusivity ("alone / by oneself").
Always used with a definite article.

/lessons/adjectives: In Greek, adjectives must agree with the noun they modify in

. This means that
            a single adjective like "good" can have many different forms.

Every adjective has three genders to match whatever noun it
            describes.

/lessons/verb-groups:
In Greek, verbs are categorized into "Conjugations" (Συζυγίες) based
            on their ending in the Present Tense. Knowing which group a verb
            belongs to is essential for conjugating it correctly.

Conjugation (Κλίση) involves changing the verb ending to match the
            person. Modern Greek verbs are grouped into four main paradigms.

/lessons/verb-to-be: The verb 'to be' is arguably the most important verb in any language. In Greek, it is irregular but follows a consistent pattern of endings.
Click the Greek phrases below to toggle their English meaning. Observe how the verb changes.

/lessons/verb-to-be.onclick: const pairs = {'Είμαι καλά': 'I am well', 'I am well': 'Είμαι καλά'}; this.innerText = pairs[this.innerText] || this.innerText;
const pairs = {'Είσαι έτοιμος;': 'Are you ready?', 'Are you ready?': 'Είσαι έτοιμος;'}; this.innerText = pairs[this.innerText] || this.innerText;
const pairs = {'Είμαστε εδώ': 'We are here', 'We are here': 'Είμαστε aquí'}; this.innerText = pairs[this.innerText] || this.innerText;

/lessons/prepositions:
Prepositions (Προθέσεις) are small, indeclinable words that usually
            come before a noun or pronoun to show relationship in space, time,
            or manner. In Modern Greek, nouns following a preposition are almost
            always in the

(to/in) almost always merges with
                the definite article that follows it:
/lessons/conjunctions:
Conjunctions (Σύνδεσμοι) are words used to connect sentences,
            phrases, or individual words. They are essential for building
            complex thoughts and flowing conversation.
/lessons/particles:
Particles (Μόρια) are small, indeclinable words that don't fit
            easily into other categories but play a vital role in Greek syntax,
            specifically in forming future tenses, subjunctive moods, and
            expressing emphasis.

You have reached the end of the Formal Grammar section of the
                Modern Greek curriculum. You now have a solid foundation in
                phonology, morphology, and basic syntax. Next, dive into the
                **Practical Phrases and Dialogues** to see all this grammar in
                action!
/lessons/interjections:
Interjections (Επιφωνήματα) are words that express sudden emotions
            or reactions. They are often used in spoken Greek to add color and
            feeling to a conversation.

is one of the most common words in Greece. Depending
                on the tone, it can mean "Come on", "Let's go", "Hurry up", or even
                "Get out of here!" (in disbelief).
/lessons/phrases:
Mastering these core phrases will help you navigate your first
            interactions in Greek. Practice saying them out loud to improve your
            pronunciation!

is extremely versatile. It means
                "please", but also "you're welcome", and sometimes "I'm sorry?" or
                "Yes, can I help you?".
/lessons/social-expressions:
This lesson covers common expressions used in social settings,
            including answering the phone and social events.

is the most common wish in Greece. It
                is used for birthdays, name days, weddings, Christmas, Easter, and
                almost every national holiday!
/lessons/dialogues-travel:
When traveling in Greece, knowing how to ask for directions and
            handle common travel situations is essential.
How do I get to the square?
How do I get to the Acropolis?
In Greece, a nod of the head
means "Yes" (Ναι). This can be confusing for visitors at first!
